That's a helluva good email, isn't it? So let's break this sucker down and see if we
can't figure out what made it tick so well. Here are my thoughts, observations, guesses and
opinions on the matter:

-- 3 of 16 --

Tun EUeIT PLAYERS NSwSLETTER
1. Tight, fascinating story
This email is an A* example of storytelling that sells. It sucks you right in from the
start (especially if you're in the market and, let's face it, even if you're not...) It seems and
looks and FEELS like content, but is really pure sales pitch. But, it's not the kind of sales
pitch that turns people off It's the kind of sales pitch anyone in the market WANTS to
get. I doubt anyone emailed Marquetta after she sent it out and complained about it. But,
a whole lot of people bought. And, at the end of the day, that's how you judge an email.
Not by its open rates, or clickthru rates or number of unsubscribes or praise mail from
people. But bv how manv people bousht from it. More: This email is a sales pitch. but it's
also informational (not educational). Meaning, yes, it tells the ingredients -- the "what".
But the "how" is actually exposed as kind of complicated. Thus, the hero (the product)
swoops in to save the day with its simplicity.
2. Mailed during holiday weekend
Some people like to fight me tooth and nail about this. And most gurus (and all
parrot squawking goo-roos) will insist you never sell during a holiday weekend. Especia\
in the summertime when supposedly nobody is buying. I call BS on that. And, I have been
calling BS on it for years. One of my most successful launches (the original "Street Smart
Email" system) was done on Independence Day weekend (2010, when the day fell on a
Sunday, so I could start the launch method I teach -- see the December 2011 back issue in
the enclosed catalog). Does this mean you should do a launch or sale on that holiday? No,
the point is holidays can be awesome times to do sales. I've done them on pretty much
every major holiday -- including Thanksgiüng, Christmas, New Years, Mámorial Day,
Labor Day and, yes, Independence Day. I'm a big fan of "zígzag marketing" -- where
you zig, when everyone else zags. And while some gurus are pounding their desks insisting
you should NEVER do sales at a specific time or whatever, I automatically do the
opposite and have never once regretted it. Arry*ay, point is, I suspect the day this email
mailed had a nice impact on sales. Especially since, contrary to Facebook magic fairy dust
and unicorn land might suggest, most people are bored out of their minds. Have nothing
going on in their lives. And, even during family BBQs, etc, are still checking their phones
and emails (cyber habits don't stop just because it's a holiday).
3. It cornes off very genuine
Let's face it: There's nary awhiff of hype, bullshit or manipulation in this email. It
4-

-- 4 of 16 --

THE EMAIL PLAYERS NnwSLETTER
readsiust like an email to a friend. This is, by the way, one reason I'm not a big fan of
emails that don't look like plain text (unless the HTML email at least looks like plain text
at a glance). That split second where the reader thinks it COULD be a personal email
(even though they know it's a list email and not personal) is often enough to get people to
at least begin reading. And, if they begin reading, and your copy is good enough, that can
be the start of them buying from you. Otherwise, flashy images, etc can look like "B-pile"
direct mail. And unless you just happen to have a burning interest in the subject line
content, to the point where it overrides your natural inclination to delete an email that's
not personal, you could be savaging your sales. Anyway, I don't know how this email was
formatted. But, if it looked plain text then that would have augmented the personal tone
of the email. I mean, really, when you read this email, can't you literally imagine her
talking to someone like that? The conversational tone is very authentic, like it was a
transcription of a recorded tatk with a secret recorder. This is something we should all be
striüng for (and I practice this as much as anyone, you cannot let yourself get
complacent).
4. Pithy
This is where the rubber meets the dough in email. In critique after critique after
critique I do for "Email Players" subscribers I have to constantly drill this one email
principle in (üat we all have a tendency to forget, yes, even yours truly): You must make
your emails pithy! Not necessarily "short." But pithy. In other words, cut out wasted
words. Rewrite sentences and paragraphs in the active voice as often as possible. And,
really think about analogies and figures of speech to "say" more in less words. (One hard
hitting figure of speech or short analogy can save you hundreds of words in tedious
explanation sometimes). This email is very pithy. There are afew words here or there that
could be cut ftut I say that about ALL emails, even my own). But overall, it's pithy. \{hich
makes it easier to read. Which, makes it easier to buy from...
S.Just the right arnount of details
One of the most solid pieces of copywriting adüce I ever done heard was from
Gury Halbert analyzinghis famous personal ad he ran in the papers to find a woman.
One of the many lessons of that ad was... details. He didn't bombard the reader with
every little jot and tittle, of course. Butjust enough details so he came off as completel)¡
genuine. And this email above is the same way. It hasJUST the right balance of details
without going on any tangents or getting too heavy. This is related to writing pithy emails.
www.BenSetde.com . Copyright 2014 Ben Settle, All Rights Reserved . Published By Settle, LLC
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 16 --

Tun EMAIL PLAYERS NnwSLETTER
Pithy means details, but ONLY the details that need to be there. Here's a handy
suggestion: When in doubt, chop it out...
6. Doesn't rnislead
OK, this is really important. More and more, people are thinking it's okay to
outright lie in emails and sales letters. For example: I've gotten in many debates with
copywriters about why theJohn Caples "piano" ad was not only misleading but
fraudulent. Why? Because it claims a fictional person actually used the proáuct to get the
result the product promises. This would be like me making up a story about a fictional
person who subscribed to "Email Players" and made a butt load of money. It'd be a lie.
Why? Because the person never existed! I am NOT sapng you can't tell parables about
fictional people to get a point across. You can do that all day long it's all good. But, don't
cralm mose ncuonal people used your actual product when they didn't. Ary*uy, I love
how this email tells a story (whether it really happened or not, but dang if it doesn't sound
real!) and it talks about the secret, but nobody claimed to use the actual product. Instead,
she says there is a product with all the ütamins, ingredients, etc that thelady with the
great hair takes. This is how it's done. People love reading emails like this and, even betteS
they like brrüns from them...
7. Twitter flaw
Okuy, this final point is not praise, but a couple flaws I saw in the email. Both are
common flaws, though, that lots and lots of people make. First, is plugging two linls in
the same email. Especia\ if this is a one shot deal. You don't **rrt thá Twitter link
competing for the link to your cash register. Secondly, I think it's a big mistake to use
email to build Twitter and Facebook's lists, but to instead use them to buil¿ yOUR list
(i.e. use it to get people to you, not to send people to other sites). We will never know for
sure. But I suspect their sales would have been even better had there been no Twitter link
for those two reasons. And BTW, speaking of flaws...
One last thought:
Gury Bencivenga is big on reading controls and world class ads and trying to figure
out how you'd improve them. He said it's one of the best ways to get really good at
copywriting. Yes, it can be tough, but it's great "workout", and I suggest * yo,r. o*,
exercise, doing that with this email (I did, I'm like you, I have to keáp my email skills

When Mailitg Daily
Works AGAINST You
A few months back, I got this question lrom one of my website subscribers. It's a
question I know a lot of people wonder about:
I am wondering if your daily marketing advice applies to
people in fields such as mine. I'm a recipe "blogger" I
guess who writes recipe ebooks and cookbooks as my only way
of supporting myself. I had turned my aweber unsubscribe
conments off for about a year and I turned them on again
recently and I'm getting a Iot of nasty complaints from
people.
I send out 1 free recipe, l- free article (sometimes has a
plug for something on amazon or one of my recipe books).
and L promotion with 1 reminder a week.
And I get so many emails from people saying I send too much
stuff they cannot read it, and then others who are
downright livid I use emails to promote my books. My
readers seem to be the 40-70 year old range, female,
overweight, sickly, cheap American who is highly offended
by anything that's not free.
Some say I should give even more free content and less
promos (how do I pay bills?) and you say send daily emails.
Not sure what to do because my readers do not like/
understand the purpose of email marketing.
Where to begin? How about from the top: Your FIRST mistake is turning on the
aweber comments function. You will get nothing but dirt flung on you from losers who
have nothing better to do (the exception being if you want to use the angry comments as

THn EMATT, PLAYERS NEwSLETTER
fodder for more emails...) Secondly, you are making the classic marketing mistake: Not
selling to buyers. There's a marketing formula that goes like this -- Your list (i.e. traffic
online) is 50% of the battle. Your offer is 30Yo. And your copy is 2OYo.In your case, you
are generating the wrong trafñc. This is your first bottleneck. Fix that and almost all these
problems disappear. I{ext, you are not properly weeding the nitwits and naysaying haters
out at the start. A lot of people poo-poo what I'm about to say (but that's okay, it's not a
crime for them to be wrong), but I think you should fill your opt in pages and boxes with
verbiage to turn off the wrong people as much as it turns on the right people. So, for
example, I tell them (1) at the opt in (2)in the welcome email and (3)periodically in my
daily emails, that my emails are DAILY Only an illiterate moron could possibly get mad
at me for sending daily emails, etc, because I have made it 100% clear that's what they'll
be getting. The result is, I can't even remember the last time someone complained about
getting too many emails from me, on any of my lists. Plus, this has the side benefit of
making Google huppy, if you choose to use AdWords, as theywant complete transparency
-- which is why on my splash/squeeze page at www.BenSettle.com it says:
I agree that when I sign up above, I will be added to a
rnarketing rnailing list where I wiII receive DAILY ernail
tips and prornotional offers frorn Ben Settle.
PIus, they have to check a box in order to opt in, too. And no, this did nothing to
reduce opt ins (at least, as far as I can tell). But, it did make the quality of my opt ins go
uP, UP, UP So aítywayyour first step is to start telling them at every opt in point and in
your welcome email what they can expect as far as frequency. The second thing you can
do (if you're not already doing this)is put a link to your product in the welcome email,
too. You don't have to be in their face about it or aggressive. But, at least put a link
passively in there, maybe in the PS. This will do two things: (1)help you nab the low
hanging fruit hyper buyers who want what you have IrIOW and (2)let them know from
the onset )¡ou are in business, and are not a charity. Someone should ask these idiots who
complain about you selling to them if they'd work for free. The answer is obüously no, so
why would you work for free? But, some people are just asses and this is what they do.
But, by selling something in that first email you set the precedent. Some goo-roos will tell
you to "incubate" new subscribers by giüng lots of great free info first, then selling later.
That's just not the way it works. All that does is train them to expect free stuff Train them
to bl¿y, instead. Yes, you will give good information (not education, but information,
there's a difference). You will be valuable to them. And, you will treat them and their time
with respect. But, you are a business. You are try-rng to serve your market by getting your

superior product into the hands of as many people you can in the most honest, ethical
and straight forward way If they don't like it, there's the unsubscribe link. Don't let the
opt out page hit ya in the ass. (You don't SAY these things, of course, it's implied by doing
what I've outlined above). Thing is, if you don't get üe list part right, and bring them in
right, and give them the right expectations then daily emails will not only not work for
you, üey will work against you (especially if they start clicking the spam button instead of
unsubscribe.)Also, as a side note, make the "from" email address auto-reply back with a
short email telling them to go to a contact form on your site to email you. This will bother
some people, but it will turn thelazy haters off from even bothering to email you at all. At
the very least, try it and see if it helps. But I know all too well how easy it is for people to
puke out their hate with a quick reply email. Make 'emjump through a hoop or two and
they may not even bother. Finally, don't give hard informatio\ away henceforth. Instead...
philosophize! This is something I'll talk more about in a future "Email Pla)rers" issue
seen. But, for now, talk about the problems the market faces. Make lists of things people
do wrong. Tell stories about people who have done great things in relation to your
product niche. Do Q&As. Talk about the health benefits of doing what you teach in your
product. Find news stories that relate to your market and just give your thoughts on them.
In other words, make 'em informational, not educational. Do all the above and methinks
you'll have way less complaints, way more sales, and lots of peace of mind.

Subject Line Ternplate
One of the single most successful sales letter headline templates I've ever used was
s]1nlr: "warning: Don't Read rhis unless-" I've used it in werything from self
defense (where it killed it for years) tobiz opp ro golf, But, I've also used iii., email subject

THE EMAIL PLAYERS NNWSLETTER
lines, too, with great results. It is based off a headline Gary Halbert wrote that said
"Warning: Do Not Read This Unless You're Already Rich". How can you NOT read
that? Rich: poor or middle class, that headline is truly irresistible. And I have found it
(and variations of it) works just as well with email subject lines when surrounded by the
usual lame subject lines your market sees every day. Some examples:
tüarning: Don,t Read This Unless You're Already Marketing Sawy
v,Iarning: Don't read thiS UnleSS you're frustrated with women
vlarning: Don't read this unless you're angry at your boyfriend
And so on. You can also play with the word "Warning" and do things like:
tüarning: Not For The Squeamish
Warning: Blatant Sales Pitch
!{arning: Blatant Testimonials
Ary*uy, it's a great subject line template you can use for any kind of product,
service, idea, or topic. Muyb. it's the word "warning" puts people on alert. Who knows?
All I know, is it works and can create great curiosity. Now, a warning (no pun heh): Do
NOT use this more than once every few months or so. If you use it too much to your list
it loses its effectiveness. I probably use it two or three times per year max. But, definitely
do use it, and see what happens. You may be pleasantly surprised...
I{ow Ib LIse A 100-Year Old Classified Ad
To Make ALL Your Clairns And Offers
66lron Clad" Believable In Your Ernails
66Men wanted for hazardous journey. Srnall wages. Bitter
cold. Long rnonths of cornplete darkness. Constant
dangen Safe return doubtful. I{onour a'.d recognition in
case of success. -Ernest Shackleton.tt
So read an ad that, from what I have been told, prompted what seemed like half of
England to respond. But why? Why would anyone respond to an ad like that? For the very
same reasons people respond when this approach is used in emails: It oozes credibilit,v,
makes a challenge and taps into some other powerful psychological forces. I mean, think

Tuo EMATT PLAYERS NnwSLETTER
about it: You have SD( negatives, followed by ONE positive benefit. Today's copywriters
would say üis can't work. And, as they usually are, táday's copywriters are wrong.
Preceding a claim with multiple negatives gives that one claim 100% betievability and
credibility. It is basically the red car in a lot full of white cars. You almost don't care about
the negatives because that one positive stands out like afartin study halt (but in a good
way). So how do you use this in emails? It;s simple: Whenever given the cirance, tJk
about the negatives of your product. Work as hard on a list of reasons people should
NOT buy as you do on your list of benefits and features. Then, in your emails, work them
in every once in a while. For example: When selling "Email Players" I'm not shy about
talking about its drawbacks. I am all about talking about the price, the amount of
content, why my system takes WORK, etc. Then, when I list a benefit or two, they are
taken more seriously and don't sound like all the other Internet marketing BS claims my
market sees every day. Even better: Think of ways to 65spin,, your negatives into
reasons to buy. I've talked about this in the hallowed pages of this newsletter before.
Sales trainer Barry Maher calls it "making the skeleton dance" -- and it's a great way to
sell. It builds huge credibility into your business and makes it very easy for people to say
"yes!" when you ask for the sale. For example:
"Yes tngfees are high, but this is uthy rny clients hire rne. I
oüer d.eliuer and thcy're notjust okay paying tnltfees,
they're hory! to pay th¿rn"
Or:
ccYes ute haae the utorst custottter seraice cornglaints in the
industry, but thatts because ute haae I0 ütnes tnore
cttstottefs, .so naturally ute uill get ¡nore cornplaints, but
ute also haae 10 timcs t lore custo¡tters becauie utetre the
tttost trusted, haae the best guarantee, yadayadayadatt
See how that works? Your homework assignment for this month is to make a huge
list of the drawbacks of your product. Then, think up ways to spin them into reasons to
buy. And then, do an email each week trumpeted that drawback and the reason why it,s
actually a reason to buy. This is an extremelypowerfirl tip and I would bet not 1 in 1000
marketers have ever bothered doing it. Which means, when YOU do it, you'll have that
much more of an advantage over them.

One question I keep getting over and over and over... is from copywriters who
want to ofler email services. They want to know if it's any diflerent writing for clients than
it is for your own stuff. And the answer is it's absolutely no different with clients other
than if they're timid and nervous nellies they may not want you to get too personal and/
or outrageous, etc. I\ot much you can do about them, though, just do your best and try to
get away with as much as you can (or find better clients with more open minds). But,
here's away that not only will make it so clients will not only not have a single problem
with what you write, but will do much of the "work" for you. It's what I did with client
work (especially if I wasn't as familiar with the market), and you can easily do the same:
l. Tafk to the product guru via phone.
2. Have a TON of questions about their personal story,
their philosophies, their interactions with people in the
rnarket prepared.
3. Malre it a LONG catt. (Don't tell thern it will be long,
that scares ern ofT.)

rrrore the rnerrien They will get LOST in their oum stories.

6. Have it tra¡rscribed. (Ideally transcribe it yourself if
you have tirne.)

I
THE EMAIL PLAYERS NBwSLETTER
the problem but vague about the solutions, and all the other tactics you've been learning
in these newsletters and in the "Email Players Playbook". Doing this took a huge load off
voice. Plus, your emails are going to sound far more authentic than if you try to ape your
client's voice and slang, etc. Try to get them to tell lots of stories. The more the merrier.
Then each story is an email. Each lesson oi inspirational event is an email. His/her
opinions on things are emails. What my friend Michael Senoff does before he interviews
people is asks them to write down 10 stories. Those become the most educational and
entertaining interviews, full of life and excitement -- just like you want your emails to be.
Another Way Ib App.ase People
Who Cornplain When You Do A Sale
For Sornething They Paid Futl Price For
Back in theJuly "Email Players" issue I talked about why you don't need to worry
about people getting mad at you for haüng a sale on products they paid full price for. And
after that issue came out, I got this interesting email from "Email Players" subscriber Nick
Quick who has a great way to not only appease someone who gets mad at you for haüng
a sale on someüing they paid fiil price for... but kinda 'Judo flip" them into being
HAPPY about it and possibly buying MORE of your products. (Tulk about making that
grinning skeleton dance!)Ary*ry, here is what he said:
I read the section on what to do when people complain about a sale and
I thought I'd share the strategy I use.
If they complain that they bought X product and now I'm giving it away
for half off, I will say something along the lines that I'm sorry that
they missed out on that special offer and i'd be more than happy to
give half off on Y product (I just look for a program they haven't yet
bought) and for good measure I'II tell them how it will complement and
put the previous purchase on crack!
wouldn't you know, a good percentage of these previous complainers
up buying more stuff!
And at the very least, it stops their complaining dead in their tracks
because you are being super cool to offer them something like this.
